Mike smexes her in the parking lot on top of her car, then leaves with, "Be at peace, Brianna Young." That's hot, huh? Be at peace?

Chapter Thirty Eight
The Honeymoon - Brianna officiates a wedding.
Spelling Errors: 2
Grammatical Errors: 2
Huh, WTF?: Oh my @&%!...
LOLs: 0
Thoughts: I can't... Ok, let me see if I can explain this one. The dude from the airport bathroom, well, he was the son of Dia Monde, the owner of the club. Her ex-husband, Craig, is a ventriloquist, who asks Brianna to officiate the marriage of Craig to his dummy. The son, Les, then locks her in a room with the newlyweds to partake in the honeymoon. Yeah, that just happened.

You know I think there is plenty enough damning evidence here why you should not buy this book. There should be no doubts if I read it or not. If you still click to buy this book...well that's on you. Good luck!
top_profane.title: Save yourselves!

top_profane.text:
Did I read the synopsis before jumping into Dick by Scott Hildreth?

Nope.

So you had no idea what Dick was going to be about?

Correct!

Do you regret your choice of reading Dick?

HECK FREAKING NO!

I know I shouldn’t continue to say “yes” to reading books, whether purchased by me or given for review, if I haven’t done the necessary research to ensure that I’ll even like the book, since it could be disastrous for both the author and me. In the case of Dick by Scott Hildreth the gamble I took really paid off, so I’m extremely happy this worked out for me. Since I’ve only read Ex-Con it wasn’t guaranteed that Dick would be something I would like, but now that I’ve finished, I skipped right over like and fell right in that forever type of love with Dick.

My first thought while reading, and when I finished, Dick was, “if the Joker and Harley Quinn were even a little bit sane and had a Robin Hood complex, Dick would definitely be their story.” Reading Dick was like watching a long awaited movie, or an episode from one of my favorite TV shows since the reading of it was like an action packed, steamy, and completely unhinged live action story in my head. Instead of reading like a story was being told, I felt like I was a fly on a wall into the lives of Dick and Jess, and it was freaking surreal – in a great way.

For the fact that I was able to relate so well wth Dick and Jess probably says a lot about the state of how sane I am, but if there’s even a small possibility of getting a story like Jess then I won’t be changing any time soon. That first moment of introduction between Dick and Jess left much to be desired but I wasn’t completely sure where any type of relationship between them would lead since not a whole lot had been revealed about the intricacies of their characters.

The prologue where we first meet Jess was a completely different woman to the one we met in chapter one, which goes to show that it takes the right person to bring the best – or worse – out of you. Dick brought out the best in Jess and vice versa. What became clear after that first date was as Jess began to realize the type of helpmeet she could be for Dick just by being herself, she helped to bring him out of the well-constructed shell he encased himself in, then making them an even better Bonnie and Clyde.

Never was there a moment when I wanted to put Dick down. I put aside the next installment in one of my favorite series for the chance to read Dick and if that doesn’t show how much I loved this book then putting myself up as a possible shoe in to play Jess if Dick ever becomes a movie should suffice. Great job Scott Hildreth…seriously.
top_profane.title: If the Joker and Harley Quinn Were a Little Bit Sane and Had a Robin Hood Complex

top_profane.text: Here's what I thought of this movie.....crap, crap, crap, crap, crap, crap, crap, crap, crap, crap, crap, crap, crap, crap, crap, crap!!!  Acting, non-existent effects, script.....nothing but CRAP!!!
top_profane.title: Flush this Crap

top_profane.text:
Jesus tap-dancing Christ... Endless Night, that's what this stupid book is. Just an endless bloody party. Seriously, let me break it down for you:

Chapter 1-4, getting to the stupid party.
Chapter 5-10 getting drunk at the party and being a mega bitch, and pissing off some voodoo priestess.
Chapter 11-12 finally leaving the party. Lots of puking. At some point, she passes out next to some hot guy, and doesn't even get a piece of that hot dick. What the hell
Chapter 13-Whatever... just this stupid fat bitch running around, crying, getting drunk, puking, trying to get away from the voodoo chick. Trying to out-bitch the voodoo bitch, which just ain't gonna happen. I mean, Dylan is a royal bitch, don't get me wrong, but this voodoo bitch has got the serious stink eye.

Then we get to the James Bond bit. You know, the bit where the voodoo bitch lays out her whole plan. She goes on about all the shiat that happened in the first book, The Scene. So, apparently, this voodoo bitch needs skanky whore blood from blond bitches in L.A. to fuel her evil empire. Who knew?

And who the hell cares? Would it be too hard to write another original story with this crazy Dylan bitch, and her trusty side kick Tatum? Why does it have to be the same fake-vampire story, with just a bit of voodoo thrown in? I don't bloody care about the dead cum dumpsters in L.A., I care about this new story in New Orleans, dammit. That's why I'm reading this book. I already read the last book, so I don't need that shiat again.

That being said, it's still a fun book to read. I mean who doesn't want to read about stupid bitches getting drunk and messed up. And, for some reason, I just love reading about Dylan going full-on bitch mode, and getting in everyone's face. It's awesome.

And hell, you just gotta love it when she busts out quips like this one: "Well, bang me with a flip flop!" Ahh Dylan... I hope you never change.

I received a free copy of this book for an honest review.
top_profane.title: Endless Night - needs to end

top_profane.text:
Major Update: As of bios revision 303, Virtualization has been enabled. A lot of my qualms with the device have been fixed. There are still issues however from my original, but the main one on virtualization is fixed.

Great laptop, it is a must buy.

----------------------------------------

The Bios entry button is F2. Just as an FYI

Summary: You get a workstation desktop for $1500, with some caveats. This machine is beyond imagination 4.5/5. But let me make one thing clear, it is ideal if you are only a windows users who uses CPU accelerated software or software that supports AMD GPU acceleration. **The Ryzen 7 1700 is the reason I'm giving this a 4.5**. If it had ANYTHING WORSE oh say an R5 and below to compete with intel's 6/4 core solutions or some U series solution, then this laptop is pure trash, I'd give it a 1/5 if I could. Terrible purchase, awful design choices, lack of upgradeability, all around bad machine at its price point. Processor = 20/10. Everything else about this laptop = 1/10, primarily its outrageous price. But then again it probably wouldn't cost this much anyways. Altogether = 9/10.

God bless ya Asus, you gave us a desktop replacement laptop in every sense of the word. Well a beta version anyways.

**Note:** If you are primarily a windows user you can skip to the pros section. If you are a linux user in any way shape or form, read ahead, except for those with intermediate/moderate linux system administration or development experience. If manually compiling drivers from the linux source kernel's staging area and loading them, and messing with Grub2 entries sounds like something you start your day off with, you can ignore. For just mainstream linux users who need it for simple tasks like using GCC, yeah read the following section. It isn't fun. **Also, use ethernet or use a USB wifi adapter, because realtek is about to ram your behind, no lube when it comes to device support**.

----------------------------

So let me rule out as of the BIOS 300 revision, KVM/HyperV is dead in the water. No AMD-V support to speak of. This is completely in software, Asus's bios does not support it. Hopefully that is fixed. So if you wanted to run Docker or VMware, good luck.

If you wanted to run it in a hard drive like a local install, get ready for some fun.

1. Secure boot will screw you. So installing any kernel version that isn't gpg checked by Microsoft will result in a very very bad time. Make sure the signatures are valid for your kernel installs, if the secure boot keys even exist for it that is.

2. **No out of box wifi support**

This is major. The realtek rtl8822be is not currently in any version of the linux kernel outside of the most recent afaik which is the 4.14 kernel version. If you roll your own, or you're a more experience user, you should be fine. If you aren't, this gets fun. CentOS will not be a fun experience, since you need ElRepo, and you need to download it before hand since you won't have internet. You can install it from the staging area of the linux kernel, or use that .bin from one of the rtlwifi folders. Just don't expect you can sudo install the driver. You're going to have to modprobe or manual compile yourself. Because if you do just use 4.14 by manually upgrading, it won't actually find any networks. But it'll show the wifi symbol, so that's an improvement over NOTHING I guess.

3. Dual booting is terrible

Grub2 will be a big pain in the rear. Especially with Windows 10 dual boot and Secure Boot on. You have to know what you are doing. Try finding and securing those GPG keys. Or editing the keys in the bios. Who honestly knows, I'm no expert in this shit.

Ubuntu 17.10 will NOT install correctly as theres a Grub2 AMD64 signature issue, that crashes the installer every single time. This is on MD5 checked Ubuntu, Lubuntu & Xubuntu, where they MAY boot once after install but after that you'll load into grub's minimal bash env.

CentOS boots fine, tested with the everything ISO. Linux 4.4 doesn't support the wifi adapter.

Linux 4.14 **should** support it, but after install, wifi can't find any networks.

I'm going to try installing the drivers from the staging area in the linux 4.14 kernel. RTLwifi, I guess I have to enable it in the .config or using make menuconfig setup. Woo. This is more sysadmin nonsense that I wish I didn't have to deal with.

What compelled Asus to use Realtek and their god awful untested no mainlined drivers is unknown to me. Was it cheapest per 100k units or whatever they ordered from? Realtek and Killer can go die in a fire.

Also whichever JoeSchmo who was part of the firmware team for this laptop, sincerely go screw yourself. Your UEFI interface is terrible. Your firmware is awful given the hilarious amount of time since this thing has launched that you've had. You haven't enabled something as necessary as virtualization. You give literally no options to configure any of the hardware outside of the peripherals, considering this is a "rog" laptop that seems really counter intuitive. Forget the fact that I can't edit any options on A DESKTOP CHIPSET. No memory timings, no processor cores, no SMT enable/disable. Fucking nothing. YOUR BOOT MENU OPTIONS ARE GOD AWFUL. You make EFI an abysmal experience to use. My first EFI motherboard was much more painless and that was like 6 years ago when it became mainstream. HOW ASUS?? HOW???

Also Realtek, you stupid ignoramuses, did you twits just develop in Windows and nothing else? Was it all in C/C++ and C#. Because this 6 months mature driver is barely functional. I lost connection to my 2.4ghz network all the time and that's on Windows. The thing that's supposed to work. THE 2.4GHZ NETWORK. No not the 5. The long range, been around for fifty centuries network. If my piece of trash Blu Android phone can connect, how can your brand new PCIe cart suck so much dirt. And what possessed you not to release into mainline kernel. YOUVE HAD HOW LONG TO DO THIS EXACTLY? Do you people not develop on any Linux machines? If I go there is it all Windows Server, Azure, Hyper-V installs and Win10 enterprise? Fat chance I have yet to see a hardware company that doesn't have a large portion of its software/firmware team working on Linux machines. SO HOW DID THIS HAPPEN? If I can find threads from as far back as August from HP's Omen X laptop where users can install it through experimental drivers. IF ITS BEEN SITTING IN STAGING FOR 10 CENTURIES IN SOFTWARE YEARS, YOU DIMWITS SHOULD HURRY IT UP.
Hey morons, maybe if you opened your piece of garbage device libraries and developer manuals, I might start with some AMD specific assembly and C. I'm not in anyway a competent or serious firmware engineer, especially not for a modern EFI cutting edge wifi adapter, but god help me this is really making it seem like the only solution is to hop on the mailing lists, debug your hardware for weeks on end, make low quality C and machine code, and when it all slapshods together, sending your entire corporation a steaming pile of my own feces, personally hand assembled by yours truly, along with a picture of a low res middle finger since you blithering idiots can't seem to do your damn jobs.

----------------------------

Pros:

- **8 Cores, the full Ryzen 7 1700 experience**
- Pretty light
- $1500 for a nonclevo brand name laptop, well engineered to include for 32GB + 8 cores with full 2 way SMT. Seriously that's an amazingly good price.
- As notebookreview/check said, speakers ain't bad yo

Cons:

- Biggest Con: Asus really needs to decide what the heck it is doing with this. Not exactly enterprise or mobile server but these are easily workstation machines, but they are seriously kneecapping workstation repurpose users. No default Virtualization support in firmware/bios for a processor that totally supports it, windows only drivers for a lot of functions, devices that area also primarily windows only, artificial limits on hardware for things like 32gb cap on ram. It has some serious bipolar issues, it cannot decide what the it wants to be. If it wants to compete with intel use an R3 or R5. Using an R7, using realtek's newest devices that aren't even that high performant with weak support, using an incredibly cut down bios with many essential features missing or disabled, limiting total system memory, using an rx580, garish color schemes and design, weird motherboard layout that flip flops between easy upgrade to infuriatingly bad design THESE ALL SEND SUPER MIXED MESSAGES ASUS. What on earth did you actually want to do with this laptop? Because it sure as heck isn't doing both. This isn't a god dang razer pro, just because you shove in an R7. Pick a side, either go gaming or go workstation, because this shit is not it. And next time, like all other business machines, include a dock port that's not through USB. I mean seriously people.

- Keyboard is kinda terrible. No seriously this thing has some god awful membranes I guess. Doesn't feel like scissor switches. I wish they were at least scissor switches. and more backlighting options, like White would be nice.
Also maybe a nice low profile switch would be good. Like I'd love full MX but I'd be very happy with ML or Kalih or w/e the new switch of the day is. The key travel on these things are so low, and that mushy feel that is somewhere between the touch keyboard from microsoft, a scoop of mashed potatoes and a sad attempt at tactility during the bottom out. I kid you not I think the microsoft surface keyboard cover for the SP4 onwards is better, and that thing is at least excusable given the form factor. But this...? Hey maybe the are scissor switches, still an atrocious keyboard imo. You get way better these days now that a ton of gaming laptops come with mechanical options. Since this is a portable desktop, just get yourself a low profile external.

- Not enough keys. Here, take a close ultrabookreviews pictures.So many keys (besides the obvious function row) have dual use through using the FN key which can be annoying for things like num lock. I'm used to a Tenkeyless, but this is seriously retarded. Like it's so middle of the road. Is it trying to give me a ton of keys? Is it just going for 104? How about 87? Even the keyboard doesn't know. What the hell were the designers thinking. You switched curlybraces for brackets in lower case? You have a rog key but you make Num Lock share with Home. And Num Lock is an FN + Home button use. WHAT THE HECK? Replace the stupid rog key with the Num lock. And how are insert and delete double placed in the keypad, but you can put page up and page down on the 8 and 2 keys. I know that's more traditional, but what the actual heck is traditional about this stupid keyboard might I ask? Also why does PgDn have its own key? PgDn is there twice, I mean do we really need PgDn twice, come on guys........BUT NOT NUMLOCK???? This layout was designed by morons. Why the unholy garbage is the power button doing as a regular button near the keypad. I mean it isn't the worst place I've ever seen, but it's still pretty terrible. You should make a power button away from the rest (you know like where that obnoxious ROG logo is), recessed and given a different feel or texture from the rest of the keyboard.

- Screen is just ok. 1080p :/ but hey freesync. It honestly doesn't get that bright, I have it at full brightness at all times. My SP3 kicks its rear, what the heck? That's a cheaper and older machine, which is literally all screen, what are you doing Asus?

- Comes stock with a 1TB HDD and some run of the mill SATA III Sandisk SSD

- Thing is kind of a pain to open up. Use a flathead like other laptops but definitely not the easiest laptop to enter.

- Linux support as of December 2017 is appalling.

- Installing RAM without heatsink removal was way too difficult. Like annoyingly so. The heatpipe is right on top of the second slot. LISTEN HERE YOU MORONS WHO DECIDED THAT WAS THE WAY TO GO? The thermal engineer? The team who decided general placement? The High Speed Digital guy who said "yeup can't have the RAM any further away from the CPU, too many reflections and transmission line issue". THE HEAT PIPES ARE OVER THE RAM, less than an inch of clearance. Don't get me started on how impossible it is to access. Usually with RAM you need to angle it at between 45 to 90 for SODIMM installs. I had to pray and just JAM it in as best as possible without hurting any of the gold contacts. Otherwise you'd have to remove 12+ sets of screws to take apart the entire heatsink, 2 fans and contacts to the CPU and GPU. Seriously, you have to take the entire thing apart to add more ram. Let that sink in. Easily a multihour job. Check the Manual, it only talks about installing M.2 and SATA drives. Most laptops I've seen have sections just dedicated for RAM upgrading. This thing is a GAMING laptop with a desktop processor and B350 chipset, not some mainstream laptop where no one will upgrade the internals. It isn't that far fetched to think they should include it.

- To give you an example of how retarded the ram install location is, an HP from 2014, a media PC that cost $800 with a 17" screen. It had a compartment just for the RAM. JUST FOR RAM. 2 screws, boom. The manual even talked about it. A media pc, with a dual core i3 and 6gb of ram. This 2017 full desktop replacement $1500 PC didn't include something that I feel is so fundamental. WHAT THE ACTUAL GARBAGE.

- Removal of the battery cable constantly sparks when you reattach which is always panic inducing

- RX 580. FFS, why not one of the new Vega's or a 1060. Or hell even a 1050 idgaf. I know the rx 580 can trade blow for blow for the 1060, but 1060 is fairly power efficient afaik and I think laptop manufacturers probably have much more experience developing a motherboard with the 1060 vs the 580.

- Trackpad is aight. But just barely. I think my old ass chromebook from Samsung's trackpad was about as good.

- Notebook Fan Controller (software) doesn't currently support this laptop. And the stock one that comes with Rog Gaming center is.......................

- **Seriously this keyboard is trash**

- No Thunderbolt 3. Asus this is 2017. Come on bruh.

- Can't easily buy a Asus 330W PSU. I believe it's the same model as the Dell 330W PSU, just with the ASUS laptop connector.

- Why doesn't this friggen goliath of a PSU have a fan on it? Did they make some ultra efficient PSU? Is this thing some 80+ Platinum that it doesn't waste any heat? Seriously for this beast of a thing a Fan should probably be mandatory on the PSU for long life.

- Garish red backlighting. Thing isn't really that bright either very middle of the road.

- 16GB Single Channel. SINGLE CHANNEL???? In this day and age come on ASUS. Couldn't they just stick with 8gb x 2 if they were going with a 16gb? Dual/Tri/Quad channel controllers are the norm these days, and the 1700 can address a ton of memory so why this weird limitation. If it can do up to 64GB, let it do 64gb. Dual Channel ASUS, 4 slots. Easy. Wut.
- 2 SODIMMS? why not 4???? Again see above.

- This beast has so much space you'd think theyd include like another HDD place, or a second NVME slot or something but no. I know, lets jam our terrible bulky battery that only gives 60 minutes of life anyways, right in the middle. Not like other manufacturers who have it removable, oh no we here at asus don't think a removable battery would ever be necessary to free up space for more hard drives. Who could possibly every want that?

- The stupid middle screws are weirdly larger than the rest and harder to take out of the case. Can easily confuse with the other screws and have fun playing guess which screw goes in which hole.

- Wish they had a better keyboard placement. Made it one of those without the trackpad or the trackpad to the side.

- **No virtualization support yet**. **I can't enable AMD-V** or smv or what have you in BIOS since as of BIOS version 300 it isn't accessible. **This is a seriously big con** :(. I have two linux virtual boxes plus I use Vagrant and Docker so this blows hard rear for right now. Like I develop on both windows and linux, this is terrible right now. I'm hoping a later bios version opens it up. I don't know if theres away to enable it in EFI but I feel like its a firmware feature locked in the BIOS ROM chip. I've contacted asus support but no dice yet. This seriously sucks.

- Can't go on American Megatrends site to get the MB ID software, doesn't seem to work.

- Since Ryzen Master won't open anymore I can't get it to OC to 4GHZ. Stuck at stock 1700 levels

- The BIOS is cut down as hell. Like the UEFI interface is shitty enough but I've seen way older laptops with a traditional almost monochrome GUIs with more features, yeesh.

- Ryzen Master isn't working. Keeps asking for 1.0.0 drivers, which there doesn't seem to be a solution for.

- I hate ROG Branding. The lights, the symbols the whole thing :|.

- ACCESSING THE RAM SHOULDN'T BE THIS HARD ASUS. IVE SEEN MANY OTHER NON GAMING LAPTOPS WITH DESIGNATED ACCESS PORTS JUST FOR HDDS AND RAM. WHY THE HECK IS YOUR HEATSINK RIGHT ON TOP OF THE RAM. WHAT A BUNCH OF TRASH.

- I wish they went for Intel NICs and the Intel 7620 wifi cart

- The Realtek card is kind of a bag of hot garbage. Updated with the latest firmware from Realtek's site

- Haven't tried the gigabit nic yet but it probably sucks too

Now you might be thinking I hate this laptop. You'd be wrong. For as many complaints I have with it. I love this thing.

For once I didn't have to think of springing $2000 for a starting notebook from Sager to get the i7 8700k or $1500 for a i7 7700 (non unlocked). Much better laptop for a way lower price.

It's a desktop to go guys. Not some miniITX crammed with a handle type of shit. My main desktop is a mATX in a tiny case, I would know. It's still not very portable. I don't even have any SATA drives on that thing and I still wouldn't call it easy to handle.

Nope a full desktop. On the go. A Desktop. On your lap. At all times.

I paid about 2k for mine but that's because I got a 1TB 850 EVO and a 16GB DDR4 SODIMM with it. And a laptop bag.

I can't get ryzen master to work anymore after the fall update :/ I need to figure out why it won't work. GPUTWEAKIT works fine and I've downclocked my RX580 to -50% power target, and I might lower it from there manually.

Ryzen Master did work before I finished updating everything which makes me sad, I have enough power and thermal headroom to OC the 1700 to a 1700X stock level.

The asus gaming center software has a fan control but holy shit is it bad. Can't figure out when it spins up or down. Only slider controls. UGH. Also can't access AMD WATTMAN or that other piece of software. Just installed the latest Chipset and GPU drivers but don't seem to be able to access a lot of the features.

Besides those caveats, this thing is computationally more powerful than my primary desktop. For my work it is so amazing. The pro's WAY outweigh the cons.

FULL DESKTOP. No Clevo. Just good ol brand name Asus, with their custom designs. And I love ASUS so I'm happy with it.

I don't know what embedded system controller it uses for the management engine, and I also didn't peek at any noteworthy ICs in it. Particularly the DC-DC converter used to Voltage Regulation on the GPU, Memory and CPU. So while I hope ASUS overspecced, I can't quite say. I hope increasing the OC and voltage respectively doesn't spike energy load on the converter and catch the damn thing on fire. But since I can't use Ryzen Master I guess we will never know.

This thing is probably an overpriced and underperformance gaming desktop. Go with fucking anything else for this price. Just stick to intel + nvidia if you're doing pure gaming.

But for any multicore work that is non GPU accelerated this thing absolutely kills it. Can't be overstated it's so liberating to have a truly portable desktop.

A "similar" clevo build from Sager Notebook is $2488 with an i7 8700 (the 65w one to match our Ryzen 7 1700) and while you get maybe a smidge more IPC you lose out on two physical cores and 2 virtual threads. That's a lot. For $1000 more. HA get real. But you do get a full GTX 1060 so there is that. If you're doing something that takes advantage of GPU acceleration yeah that laptop would make much more sense. I have 32gb DDR4, 1TB ssd, 256gb SSD, a desktop class midtier GPU, and an 8 core CPU. Now try that on Sager notebook, over 3k easy.

Otherwise stick to this. I don't think a whole heck of a lot of applications are GPU accelerated in my limited knowledge compared to how many have multicore support.

Seriously I can run a linux VM with KVM support through VBOX and assign it 8 threads, and take no performance impact. I can literally run 2 desktops at once, each with 4 core 8 thread 16gb of ram each. This is ridiculous. Thing weights 7 some odd pounds but gives you two laptops at the minimum in one. Holy hell.
While I wait for kernel compile I can go back to designing in windows. When that's backed up, back to linux. When I'm running a sim I can just shut everything down and schedule all priority to that application. Well I mean this is ideal, since VM still doesn't work. But right now I can run Handbrake, while running a 1080p movie in the background and doing development work, and it doesn't even flinch. Considering handbrake uses all cores you'd think that'd be an issue but no.

ON THE GO. Anywhere with a plugpoint. That could be literally anywhere, Planes, airports, starbucks who cares. You don't need your companies desktop (outside of their software licenses) to do serious work anymore. Like just think about how awesome that is! Blows my friggin mind.

What I think Asus should have done?

- Better laptop motherboard, increase to 18.4" if necessary, but give more space for easy upgrades
- 2 NVME and 2 Sata drive bays
- 4 SODIMM slots. Default dual channel with up to 64GB support.
- Blu ray drive
- Get rid of the RX580 and take some cheapo GPU OR a Firepro/Quadro card.
- Increase all thermal priority to the processor
- Intel networking equipment (Nic and WAP)
- Include an unlocked 1800X or 1700X
- Come with either an NVME drive or dual SSDs
- Put a fan on the PSU
- Increase resolution to either 1440p, 1600p or 2160p
- More keys
- Better keyboard
- Just get rid of the stupid trackpad
- Make the keyboard a full travel keyboard like that MSI one with cherry MX keys and the 87 keyboard layout that sits at the very front, not in the middle of the laptop.
- Decouple the power button from the keyboard cable.
- VGA would be nice, or full width DP. If you go mDP just go TBOLT.
- Remove ROG. This is a workstation laptop masquerading as a gaming notebook. This should go against Thinkpads and ZBooks. Not billion LED gaming machines ffs.
- Put in 2 TB3 ports.
- Fan control software

Obviously no one really gives a hoot about these recommendations, but hey I thought I'd voice my concerns.

So I needed a new laptop. Well not really but I wanted one. I've had an i5 SP3 since release and a desktop with an i7 6800k, GTX 1080 and twin Intel and Samsung NVME drives.

So I didn't exactly have a reason to get a new laptop.

But I did.

And I got the now famous 8 core machine.

So far it's been pretty good. All in all I'd give it a 4.5/5.

How I have it set up:

- Ryzen 7 1700 @ base clock
- RX 580 @ -50% power target, otherwise stock
- 1TB Samsung EVO 850 with Windows 10 Home 64bit with the fall creators update
- 256gb base Sandisk OEM SSD with Xubuntu 17.10 (not working)/CentOS 7.1 with 4.14.16 kernel (working, no wifi)
- 32GB Ram (Samsung OEM + Micron/Crucial @ 2400mhz)
- Whatever panel Notebookcheck said running @ 1080p
- USB2 built in webcam (disabled in bios)
- Realteck NIC (OEM)
- Realtek Wifi/BT combo PCIe cart rtl8822be (OEM). I believe that this is soldered in, since on the on the Realtek site it just says what chip package it comes in. I wish it was an mPCIe or mSata or something where I can plop in a nice Intel NIC and an Intel wifi cart. :(. Still not Qualcomm/Killer, what a bunch of crap that is so I guess that's a plus.
- Stock charger (19.5V, 16.9A)

I've additionally got:

- USB - C (3.1) Hub
- USB3 SD/Microsd card reader
- Dell u2311h 1080p monitor through mDP -> DP

I use it with:

- Leopold FC660C Tenkeyless
- Realforce 23U Keypad
- Logitech MX Master Gen 1
- Elgato Stream Deck

Software usage:

- LTSpice
- Kicad
- SpiceOPUS
- QUCSStudio
- NGSpice
- Fritizing
- Quartus II Lite
- Vivado Lite
- ModelSim PE Student Edition
- OBS Studio
- Spyder 3
- Davinci Resolv
- WXMaxima
- SystemC + TLM development
- Icarus Verilog
- Verilator
- RStudio
- Scala IDE
- Code Composer Studio
top_profane.title: Fantastic workstation laptop, overpriced gaming laptop

top_profane.text:
Crap crap crap crap crap crap crap crap crap crap product. Horrible product. Save your money and buy something else. ANYTHING else.

I ordered this product for my Galaxy note. A teeny tiny screen that does not fill the entire screen came. it looked like an s3 screen. OK, they got my order wrong. Strike 1. Luckily when I ordered some cases another seller had thrown in some free cheap protectors. The $.99 protectors went on beautifully. No bubbles at all.

I returned the wrong screen and had amazon send me the right screens. A day later I had screens that actually fit the note! YAY!

Now time to put them on. I put the first screen on. Bubbles galore! I've places screens on my previous phone and on my nook, never had a problem. This one had lots of little bubbles. Tried to squeegee them off but that's just not happening.

Ok, so maybe this one is more sensitive then previous screens. Tried the 2nd screen that's included. Crap Crap Crap Crap Crap. Bubbles just pop up under the surface. Not one bubble like most screens but tons of bubbles. This screen is complete and utter crap!

Currently I'm on screen 2 of the cheapies, again, bubble-less and working fine. Why is a $.99 screen better then a $15 screen? Don't waste your money on these screens buy a $.99 cent pack.
top_profane.title: Horrible screen!  If I could put in a - review I would

top_profane.text:
I'll admit I bought this one without having a clue who Dick Domane was. In fact I bought it because the Map City label was a known quantity to me - the bands Purple Image and Yesterday's Children recorded for Map City. The amazingly ugly pea green vomit cover art also caught my eye, though not in a good way. Yes, it made my list of ugliest cover art ...

I don't claim to know a great deal about Dick Domane. Born Richard P. DiDomenico, he seems to have made his recording debut with a 1960 single for the small Warwick, Rhode Island based Wye label:

- 1960's 'Alone' b/w 'You're So Conceited' (Wye catalog number 5-1006)

I'm not sure where they fit in the discography, but there were a couple of other early-1960s singles:

- 'Make Me Yours' b/w 'Dearest Lee' (Joy catalog 45-231)
- 'This Is Goodbye' b/w 'Don't Pity Me' (Almont catalog number 312)

By the late 1960s he was a member of the Rhode Island based horn outfit The Blue Jays who recorded a 1970 LP "Nascence" for Map City. That seems to have somehow led to a solo deal with Map City, resulting in the release of 1970's cleverly-titled "Dick Domane". Working with producer Pete Anders and most of The Blue Jays, the album showcased a largely original set of material with a heavy emphasis on late-1960s-styled pop. While some dealers have tried to label this as psych, with the exception of the opening track 'I'm Only Dreamin'' and 'Bad Dream' it really wasn't. True, tracks like 'Saturday Morning' had a modest rock edge and included tasty dollops of fuzz guitar, but this wasn't a psych, or rock album, rather was clearly a pop collection. That certainly wasn't meant as a slam since I'm a big fan of pop and to his credit Domane had a talented for penning commercial melodies. He also had a nice and highly commercial voice that was easily as good as AM popsters like Tommy James, Gary Puckett, or Dennis Yost.

- Easily one of the best songs on the album, 'I'm Only Dreamin'' managed to blend a great sunshine pop melody with heaps of psychedelic effects including fuzz guitar, backward tapes, lysergic-flavored orchestration. A fantastic song and sad that little else on the LP came close. rating: **** stars
- 'Such An Early Hour' was a surprisingly funky country-rock number. Image a really good Brewer and Shipley song and you'll have a feel for the song. rating: *** stars.
- 'Since You Left Me' showcased the AM radio side of Domane's repertoire. A mid-tempo pop song, this one could easily have been mistaken for a Gary Puckett and the Union Gap track. Great it you liked that genre, but probably had limited appeal for psych fans. rating: ** stars
- Apparently written and previously recorded by The Blue Jays, 'I Can't Imagine' was a glistening slice of harpsichord-propelled sunshine pop, The song would have made Curt Boetcher, or The Monkees quite proud. Super commercial and radio friendly it's surprising this one wasn't tapped as a single. rating: **** stars
- 'Complete with Association-styled backing harmonies, 'Hang On' was another slice of AM top-40 pop. Probably too saccharine for most listeners, it was still better than much of the stuff you would have heard on the airwaves back in 1970. rating: ** stars
- Kicked along by a crunching John Vastano fuzz guitar, 'Bad Dream' was the album's most rock oriented performance and easily the standout track. You can only imagine what an album full of this stuff would have done. rating: ***** stars
- Complete with a tasteful horn arrangement and some nice bass from Bob Fiocco, 'Sane One' found Domane taking a stab at a more bluesy sound. Roughing up his voice a bit, the results were surprisingly enjoyable. rating: **** stars
- Starting out as a pretty ballad 'Fragmented People' quickly morphed into a fuzz-guitar powered pop song that also happened to sport the collection's weirdest lyric. I've listened to the song dozens of times and don't have a clue what this one was about (enforced conformity ?), which probably helped explain why I like it so much. Bob Fiocco provided a nifty bass line while Domane turned in another great vocal on this one. rating: **** stars
- 'Hey Don't You Know' returned to power-pop territory bearing a passing resemblance to something Tommy Roe might have recorded. Very commercial and radio-ready. rating: **** stars
- The lone cover tune, the ballad 'Maybe' had a distinctive early-1960s feel. Imagine a good Righteous Brothers track and you'll have a feel for the general sound. I will tell you that even though the song was rather pedestrian, Domane turned in a killer vocal. rating: *** stars
- This comparison will probably sound stupid, but to my ears the ballad 'Sunday Morning;' sounded like a cross between Procol Harum and a blue-eyed soul outfit like The Classics IV. Not sure why I liked this one so much, though Vastano's soulful guitar didn't hurt at all ... rating: *** stars
- The horns returned for 'The Sun Never Shines' which was another decent power pop number, this time with a bubble-gummy edge. rating: *** stars

No way to avoid the fact that this is one weird little album. Too poppy for psych fans and too psych for pop fans. Combined with the hideous cover its no surprise that this one disappeared in an instant. Still it's worth checking out since you can still locate copies at a reasonable price.

"Dick Domane" track listing:
(side 1)
1.) I'm Only Dreamin' (Dick Domane) -
2.) Such An Early Hour (Dick Domane) -
3.) Since You Left Me (Dick Domane) -
4.) I Can't Imagine (Dick Domane) -
5.) Hang On (Dick Domane) -
6.) Bad Dream (Dick Domane) -

(side 2)
1.) Sane One (Dick Domane) -
2.) Fragmented People (Dick Domane)
3.) Hey Don't You Know (Dick Domane - John Vastano) -
4.) Maybe (Richard Barrett) -
6.) Sunday Morning (Dick Domane) -
4.) The Sun Never Shines (Dick Domane) -

Domane was subsequently a member of the New York based horn band White Water who recorded a 1973 set "Out of the Darkness" for RCA Victor.

I've never heard them but Domane has also released a couple of private press albums:

"A Little Bit of This and a Little Bit of That" and "In the Clouds"

He also has a small website and myspace presence at:
[...]
top_profane.title: review

top_profane.text:
Geoffrey Sanborn achieves a great deal in this compact book, not the least of which is providing for many readers a fresh basis for engaging with Melville's 'Moby-Dick'.

Sanborn, an English professor at Amherst College, launches his effort with advice he provides his students, "Moby-Dick wasn't meant, I tell them, to be a rigorous, depleting experience, a triathlon for the homebound. It was meant to be a stimulant to thought and feeling, it was meant to make your mind a more interesting and enjoyable place" (p. 2).

Authors such as Hannah Arendt and Toni Morrison are cited and effectively used to open the veins of Melville's work to discovery. Other Melville works are productively mined, as well.

In the process, Melville is transformed from the author of a long, rambling, impious tale holding a deep secret known only to the cognoscenti into a daring novelist who succeeds marvelously at portraying life full of its physical and human complexity. (fn1) Indeed, just as a person learns and adapts from life experiences, so the devotee of 'Moby-Dick' is urged to learn and adapt to re-readings of the masterpiece.

In his book 'Quixote: The Novel and the World', Ilan Stavans provides this definition of a classic, "... a book that is a mirror in which we see ourselves reflected; a book that accumulates interpretations; a book in which we meet readers not only from the present but also from the past and the future." 'Don Quixote' and 'Moby-Dick', both open to nearly endless discovery, easily meet this definition. (fn2)

Sanborn quotes the critic Elizabeth Savage, "[Moby-Dick's] writing really requires a different reading practice, one that surrenders to its impossible abundance and honors its purposeful uncertainty, as well as its disruptions of narrative structure ..." (p. 53). The point seems to be not to solve the puzzle, but to immerse oneself in the puzzle for the sheer delight of the experience.

'The Value of Herman Melville' confronts the question, "What is 'Moby-Dick' about?" and it seems to answer: it is about human experience and our capacity for perception, perspective, and understanding ("our capacity" properly captures the sense of both possibility and, ultimately, limitation). 'Moby-Dick' is about an observant and thinking person - certainly the author, as well as, ideally, the reader - confronting 'Moby-Dick' and, indeed, the world, in order to marvel at beauty, as well as to wrestle with the metaphysical questions at the heart of both. (fn3)

Whether you are reading Moby-Dick for the first time or approaching it for the nth time, Sanborn's book is a helpful resource. (fn4)

Footnotes:

(fn1): In one of his terrific essays ("Dimensions of 'Moby-Dick'), Harrison Hayford focused upon Chapters 102 and 103 of 'Moby-Dick' that recount Ishmael's attempting to measure the whale skeleton in Arsacides. Ishmael's task is interrupted by priests who object to his making his own measurements ("Dar'st thou measure our god! That's for us").

Hayford writes (facetiously, perhaps): "Can these priests be the official Melville scholars, the Phd.s, the American literature professors, the academic guardians of true interpretation ... ?" He later adds, "... I must be careful to point out that all this insistence of mine on the book's inexhaustibleness does not mean one reader's reading of 'Moby-Dick's' dimensions is as good as another's."

(fn2): Melville, of course, was influenced by Cervantes (although there is no evidence that Melville read 'Don Quixote' prior to 1855, four years after 'Moby-Dick' was first published). Melville referred to Don Quixote as "the greatest sage that ever lived" ... high (and revealing) praise for a fictional character.

(fn3): The question "What is Moby-Dick about?" is a variation of one of the book's themes, i.e., "What is a whale?" or, perhaps, "How is it that we come to know what a whale is?" (or, more broadly, "What are the means and limits of human comprehension?"). These questions give obvious purpose to the cetology chapters. Ahab declares that we go further, however, "Hark ye yet again—the little lower layer. All visible objects, man, are but as pasteboard masks" (ch 36). Once we come to an understanding of a whale, we must ask again, "What really is a whale?" What does the whale represent?

(fn4): Geoffrey Sanborn is also the author of 'Whipscars and Tattoos: The Last of the Mohicans, Moby-Dick, and the Maori' (2011), in which a compelling case is made that accounts of certain New Zealand Maori chiefs influenced both James Fenimore Cooper and Herman Melville. The insight provides an alternative basis for understanding the characters Magua and Queequeg, as well as supports conjecture as to the composition of 'Moby-Dick'.
top_profane.title: The Marvel of Melville Reimagined

top_profane.text:
First, be aware that this edition contains the ENTIRE output of Leman's fantasy. It is a short (15 tales in all) but powerful output and as Jim Rockhill states in his preface to the book one can only wonder what might have been had Leman produced more. In a review of his book in Newman and Jones' "Another 100 Best Horror" the person (whose name escapes me) gives Leman the award of "best horror writer no one has ever heard of." It is an unfortunately accurate title.

*Warning - Multiple Spoilers*

"Window"
One thing you find out about Leman right off the bat is that just when you think you've figured out the twist to the story, you're almost certainly wrong. This story starts off with Gilson, a US government official, coming to an isolated, remote military base to clean up a mess that has been made. He arrives at the site and sees a house. But. The house looks like something out of the antebellum South, and though it is dark where Gilson stands, the sun shines from an unknown source on the house. There are people there as well. Apparently, it is a window to another time (think you got it figured out, right?). Gilson has little information and the story he comes to learn involves the research of a scientist Culvergast, who was working for the government to discover spells. However, something went wrong and Culvergast and his lab disappeared with this window the only thing remaining in its place. In setting up the plot, Leman is concise, sharp, and devastatingly effective. It turns out that this window has an opening every 15 hours and 20 minutes (they throw colored ice cubes into the opening to confirm this). Yet, before the government can descend on this window (to study and dissect it), one of the more zealous (and nostalgic) of the men, Reeves, does something incredibly stupid. He jumps in through the window. Initially, everyone is worried that time has changed (perhaps the Civil War was averted or Lincoln was assassinated in his first term). Nothing happens. And here is where you really find out what kind of writer you are dealing with when you read Leman.
I should note that I don't scare easily. This, hopefully, isn't just my innate arrogance talking. I actually wish I did. Unfortunately, I come from the generation that has been inured to "normal" horrors via slasher films and CNN. Having said that, I want to tell you that Leman managed to scare the hell out of me about half a dozen times within this book. The Window was one of them. When that little girl flashes her "smile," and then goes after Reeves, I cannot describe how scared that made me on multiple levels. A f*#king shiver went down my spine when I read that part of the story. It's a window alright, but not to another time, to another dimension where evolution apparently took a drastically different turn.

"The Tehama"
For some reason when I read this, it brought up memories of all that was great about the work of Manly Wade Wellman. I think Silver John would have been right at home in this story. The plot is simple enough. Gordon wants to kill his aunt (grandaunt technically) so the logical solution is to conjure forth a Tehama (a very nasty golem like creature with claws and sharp teeth and the whole nine) to kill her. On their first attempt it goes wrong (poor German Shepard), but Gordon remains dedicated to persevere like a devil. But, kind of like in a Wellman tale, we have a hero, Smithers. Smithers is what you would call biculturated, equally at home in the business world of the Anglo's but remaining true to his Native American roots. This is good for Gordon's aunt as it ultimately comes down to Smithers and Gordon in the final scene. Apparently Gordon failed to do his homework and did not realize there was something worse than a Tehama. A truly outstanding close to a Biblical justice ending.

"Industrial Complex"
This is another one where I think I got it figured out and then realize I absolutely do not. In the first page we find out Stanley Scott is insane, or thinks he is. The President of Consolidated Pipe and Tube Corporation, G.G. Scranton, ends up being the focus of his paranoia. He has to kill him, stop him, and those who work for Scranton as secret conspirators. You think you got it figured out again, huh? Since I ruined "Window" for you, I'll let you find out the rest. And no, it's not that old saying, "It's not paranoia if they really are out to get you." It's a much more subtle and sophisticated twist on the paranoia theme.

"The Pilgrimage of Clifford M."
I heard that with this story Leman succeeded in creating the best vampire story in the last 30 years. To be honest, THIS story was the reason I bought the book in the first place. I didn't believe it could be THAT good. After I read it I changed my opinion. With the exception of "I Am Legend," this is the best vampire story written in the latter half of the 20th century, definitely the best one from the 60's up to today. This story quite simply blew me away with its vision, characters, and story structure. It is one of those stories that you finish reading and say, "I will NEVER be able to write anything even approaching how good this is."
This story is written in the style of an article submitted to an academic journal and for some reason this mode makes it strikingly effective. There is a sterile yet horrible atmosphere to the prose, like reading transcripts of the Nuremberg trials. The narrator first discusses the common misconceptions of vampires and vampirism, noting some disturbing biological inconsistencies between fact and legend. The narrator then goes on to describe the case of Clifford M., a creature not human and not monster. Clifford was "discovered" in a nest in Comber County, where Virginia, Kentucky, and Tennessee meet by one Ossie Fimber, a 14 year-old boy. Ossie kept Clifford and for quite some time Clifford was known as "Ossie's monkey" via a local newspaper story. Shortly after that story, he broke out of his cage and killed Ossie. Not knowing the ways of his kind (Ossie having destroyed the vampire nest), Clifford remained in the forests, and was found by a party of hunters much in the tradition of the Wild Boy of Averignon. And similar to that child in France, attempts were made to "educate" the boy.
The Reverend and Mrs. Crockett spent the next few years (decades) attempting to teach the boy, whom they named Harry, how to be a civilized member of society. Ultimately, the boy, a month before his graduation day from school, exsanguinated his "mother," Mrs. Crockett, and left. There is a surprising lack of judgment in the recounting of these murders; if indeed you wish to name them that. After this, we see the first use of the name, "Clifford M." when he enrolls in Corinthia College and then two years later when he graduates cum laude from Harvard University. After this, Clifford amasses a truly large fortune and spends the rest of his life basically trying to figure out what he is (puberty having hit him like a ton of bricks and bringing along with it many troubling physical changes). His decades of investigation lead him to Sturkeyville in Goster County (a locale Leman will make use of again and again throughout his tales similar to the way Lovecraft uses Arkham). At this point in the tale, the characterization is so subtle it is almost invisible. Leman has built up a character that now looks like what a suave, rich, traditional, archetypal vampire. Perhaps even Clifford believes this. But. It all changes when he meets his kin, the real vampires. He is appalled. He cannot accept that these abominations are the foundation of his being. And in the final passage of the story, we find some of the most moving prose in all of horror fiction, the kind that blurs the line between monsters and heroes.

"Change of Address"
There was a beauty in this story that one would never have expected from the initial pages, the way Kafka began "The Metamorphosis." Willis Barley's life has just fallen apart. His daughter left to the slums with a guitar player, his son announced he's homosexual, and his wife left with a sleazy salesman. To top it off, he's just lost his job and discovered drinking. Until, literally, a beautiful woman walks into his place. Then it's his high school English teacher. At that point, Barley is convinced he's gone insane. The being explains. It is from another universe. The being knows Barley has planned to kill himself, so it offers to fulfill his wish in exchange for his body as it has work to do in this universe. Barley realizes he doesn't want to go just yet and so the "alien" (whom Barley eventually comes to call Fido) agrees to be a cohabitant in Barley's mind. And there it begins. Here's the premise. Fido is from another "universe," a place of perfection and absolute harmony. If there is any disturbance in that harmony, then it ripples, not only in that universe, but possibly in our own. The principle is that if the absolutely perfect is imperfect all that was built upon it falls down along with it. It only takes a single chink in the armor. Unfortunately, a being from Fido's universe has come to our universe and is hiding out here. That beings absconding to our universe has created the chink in the armor. It is Fido's job to find that being and bring him/her/it back, thereby repairing the chink. He needs Barley to help him (the choice being completely arbitrary as Barley was the first one Fido ran across in choosing a body to use). The journey Barley and Fido take in their search is a true odyssey. It's final denouement is, to me, the most realistic portrayal of what a hero must truly face when the final moment of truth comes. It is beautiful and poetic and once again, a powerful piece of writing by Leman.

"Skirmish on Bastable Street"
This was actually the only story in the collection I did not like. This story involves Garft, Lurlene, and Billy and a wish from a demon. As Leman says, "None of the three ever grasped the magnitude of their accomplishment, and indeed they very soon forgot what had actually happened, but they had in truth won a laudable small victory in an ancient war, and the facts of the affair deserve to be recorded." It appears that Garft's ancestor had released a demon long, long ago and was granted three wishes, two of which he made. The last, however, was never made and according to some kind of demon interdimensional statutes that wish passed on to his progeny. As this debt was not collected (and as the demon world was having its equivalent of an audit around the mid-20th century), the demon who originally owed the debt came to grant the wish. There was nothing about the story in and of itself that I disliked as Leman has other stories which combine humor and the supernatural (Unlawful Possession and How Dobbstown Was Saved). The only way I can describe it is that the story read like a Jack Finney novel, all of which (with the exception of The Body Snatchers) I cannot personally stand. Maybe this is a personal quirk and I'm being a bit too harsh on this story; however, it seemed to be glib without being engaging (like Finney).

"Olida"
This was another story that scared the living crap out of me. This story takes us back to Goster County (where Sturkeyville is located) in Grill's Fork. The Olida of the title is Olida Selkirk, a name detested and despised in Goster County, which is why Charles' Aunt Felicia speaks to him about her. It appears that Charles' cousin Dick is going to marry Olida and we can't have a Wagner marrying a Selkirk. This isn't solely an elitist tendency on Aunt Felicia's part. The name Selkirk has come to be equivalent with dishonesty, cruelty, and degeneracy it is true. In addition to this, Aunt Felicia, as well as Charles, thinks that Dick is a good mark for a con due to his overall inexperience with matters of the opposite sex, so it then becomes (on the surface) a matter of economics. So Charles goes and talks to Dick. Dick knows why he's there (his mom sent him to talk him out of marrying Olida). Dick then proceeds to go in a very roundabout way (at least it appears that way at first) of discussing the issue. He asks Charles to remember a summer years ago when they were fifteen and took a motorcycle ride down to Grill's Fork. They went to get a Coke in the local store and that's where they first met Olida, who was an older, more sexually mature girl. She eventually invited them over to her house and took a particular interest in Dick. This is where it becomes truly sinister. Charles could not remember their experience in that house either then or now past the point where they drank a cup of tea. The entire ambience of this part of the story is reminiscent of repressed memories in sexually abused children and as Charles states when he and Dick are speaking now as grown men, "What's really odd is that I don't remember forgetting. You know, why didn't it worry me the next day? I can't remember even thinking about it. I tell you, it's crazy." Dick proceeds to fill in the blanks. The tea incapacitated Charles. Olida took Dick and gave him his sexual awakening. She then showed him the "family." Apparently all the Selkirk's live to long old ages. We're talking centuries, not years. The first of the Selkirk's, Ashmole Selkirk, was here even before the Native Americans and has been the progenitor of all, including Olida. Unfortunately that longevity comes with a price (doesn't it always) of physical deformities (think of an albino Jabba the Hut . . . see told you it was scary as crap). This is the background you obtain from the story. The plot, however, is just as bizarre and extremely circuitous. It made me feel more like I was reading a mystery novel where I don't know who is the criminal and who is the victim. Olida says, when confronted by Charles, that she wants out. She is the one who fills in all the holes and lets us know exactly the depth and breadth of the evil in Grill's Fork. But, as I said all is not as it seems and the ending of the story is breathtaking in its horror, complexity, and narrative power.

"How Dobbstown Was Saved"
I have to admit that this story was just plain fun. Do not let that, for an instant, imply that it detracts from the story. This is a story that was made for fans but can simultaneously be read with enjoyment by those completely new to fantasy, sci-fi, or horror. This is also a story original to this edition, the last one Leman ever wrote.
The premise is that a mad scientist has been tampering with forces unknown and forbidden and, of course, lets loose a malevolent force of evil upon an unsuspecting town, on Halloween night no less.
Leman accomplishes multiple things here. One is a fun to read fantasy/sci-fi/horror story. The second is an homage to his roots. The last is only appreciated by the true fan and that is taking us back to the beginning. I think Rockhill makes the observation that the "golden age" of sci-fi is 12. All of us remember being a child and reading a story that captured our imagination. If we were to return to that tale later in adulthood (as I did with my favorite tale) we might find it lacking. The tale hasn't changed; we have. No man steps in the same river twice, right? We have lost the ability to see through those eyes of wonder and awe we had as children, where even the mundane was fantastic. Speaking for myself, I got a little of that back when I read this tale. And, I think that is Leman's greatest accomplishment with this story.

"The Time of the Worm"
Okay, this story from start to finish scared the hell out of me. I know Leman eschews genre classifications but this is a straight horror tale (if it walks like a duck and quacks like a duck, you know). There were points in other stories here that scared me (e.g., in "Window" and "Olida") but Leman managed to disturb me long after I finished this story. Maybe I don't have as thick a skin as I thought (said the man who read all six of Barker's Books of Blood and didn't bat an eye). It may be the same reason Ligotti scares the hell out of me (the stories, not the guy himself). This story is about an entity, the worm, taking over control of the protagonist and his father. You get a very guttural feel for what it would be like to have a lifetime of freedom gone, to regain it one day, only to lose it again.

"Bait"
I believe that this was the first story Leman ever published based on the very perspicacious logic "hey, if that guy I just talked to can get published, maybe I should try." This is also another one of those "I think I figured out the plot" stories that you will be wrong about. The basic premise: a door to door salesman is trying to pitch a "key to a younger you" book to a woman. When she asks how he acquired this knowledge, Leman starts to work his magic. In the end, yes, it is a con, but what that con is exactly is brilliant.

"Loob"
Even after reading it and having had plenty of time to digest it, I still remain ambivalent towards this tale. Not to its merit as good fantasy fiction; I think it is outstanding. My ambivalence is centered on the character of Loob. I don't know if I loathe him, pity him, fear him, feel the greatest compassion towards him, or wish him the most certain death. What I find outstanding in this tale (well, one of the things) is that I believe Leman has vocalized each one of these feelings in the narrator. And, like me, the narrator remains ambivalent in what he feels towards Loob. This story takes us, once again, to Goster County. The narrator "Tom Perkins," tells us all about Loob and his very special gift/curse. Loob is, to the mass of humanity, mentally retarded. But, he is far from helpless. In fact, he possesses a god-like power to see and effect change across space and time, altering dimensions at a whim without understanding of his power. It is this power that has affected the narrator. You see, Loob did a very, very bad thing, though he had no recognition of its moral, ethical, and societal ripples either before, during, or after the act. His act cascaded across space and time, causing the bone-chilling slaughter of several people, which in turn dominoed across time to alter the present reality. Finishing this story I simultaneously felt what I still feel, the greatest pity and the greatest hatred for Loob, perhaps the greatest ambivalence towards Loob. It is as complex a work of fiction as any I have encountered. It is truly a masterpiece.

"Unlawful Possession"
We stay in Goster County, in Sturkeyville specifically, and have a glimpse into the lives of Meg Hawthorne (witch by occupation) and Fred Watkins (realtor by occupation, murderer by accident). Fred's wife, Edna, had been possessed by a demon many years prior to him meeting Meg. Fred, not versed in such matters, simply thought she was losing her mind. His concern peaked the night he found human bones in the basement, bones that the demon, who now inhabited his wife's body, had buried. The woman snuck up on Fred and he either through courage or impaired reason sent a shovel into her head. Believing he had killed his loving wife (and not a demon), he buried the body with the other bones and stayed in the house for fear of any other person finding out his crime. His wife was not missed as several people saw her with other men (who unbeknownst to them would eventually become bones in the basement) around town late at night; so they assumed she just ran off. In enters Meg Hawthorne who loves the now dilapidated house which looks perfect for a witch. Using her powers, she makes Fred sign the house over to her. To her surprise, she finds the "ghost" of Edna in the house. Unfortunately for the "ghost," Meg knows the rules and recognizes that this is not Edna but the demon that possessed her. Apparently they both work for the same firm (I swear to God that's how they say it) and if a demon possessing a body dies in that body, they become trapped; which is quite embarrassing and a career killer for a demon. So now there is a problem. Meg knows that she can know use the demon ANY way she wants; he's basically her slave since this is now her house and the demon is quite unhappy about that. Fred is absolutely petrified that someone will find his wife's bones in the basement (as well as all the other bones, upgrading him from wife killer to serial killer based on that damning evidence). The demon decides to "help" Fred and concocts a plan to get Meg out of the way and get himself free. The rest of the story is both humorous and scary. How does Leman do that?

"Come Where My Love Lies Dreaming"
Leman tells us that there are houses that hate and houses that love. We, in the horror genre, hear often about the houses that hate. Leman gives us a story about a house that loves. This, to me, has always been a fascinating concept. Horror readers are familiar with what happens when fear, terror, and disgust are taken to extremes. But, what happens when love, joy, and bliss are taken to extremes? Is this not every bit as chilling? Before you answer in the negative, take a moment to think about the bliss and joy a drug addict feels. Would not witnessing this abominable bliss be truly terrifying (particularly in someone close to you)? I believe E.H. Visiak in "Medusa" introduced this concept (and did so VERY poorly). The only writer I know who I can say has mastered it is Robert Aickman (I'm thinking specifically of his story, "The View"). I believe Leman is the only other one who has mastered the concept of horrifying happiness (for lack of a better term). When Knapp's wife, Sally, manifests herself, it is a truly horrible image.

"Instructions"
Number 1 - You will walk outside your house. This is a horror story written in second-person in step-by-step list format. The only other person I have ever seen successfully pull off such a premise is Richard Christian Matheson and even he did not achieve the level of cosmic terror the permeates this story. I really cannot give an adequate description as the story only works with interaction (it is, after all, written entirely in the second-person so YOU have to read it).

"Feesters in the Lake"
A horror story. Period. Dear God, the man knows how to write a horror story. This, again, is another story that scared the living s*^t out of me. While not maintaining that same constant, suffocating terror that "The Time of the Worm" did (and to be honest, I didn't think it was possible to maintain CONSTANT terror in a tale from first word to last word until I read "The Time of the Worm") it is an absolutely terrific horror tale. This is Leman's nod of the hat to Lovecraft and like all good nods of the hat to the Providence sage it never errs on the side of caricature. We make our final visit to Goster County and its Arkham-like town of Sturkeyville. We begin with Nick's reminiscence of his Uncle Caleb's story about the "feesters" and Captain Elihu Feester who in the mid-19th century made a voyage through the South Pacific, was stranded on an island with his crew (where things went very bad), and brought back some sort of parasite. Captain Feester moved from Boston to Sturkeyville after a scandal (the ship he sailed on through the South Pacific burned a few miles outside Boston Harbor with all the dead "infected" crew on board). He found a wife, Ezra Stallworth, bought a small piece of land near Howard's Lake, and settled down, having four daughters: Clio, Thalia, Urania, and Polyhymnia (named after the four Muses). Unfortunately, Feester had not been untouched by the "infection" and his daughters began to change, each one earlier than the next. Their arms would fuse to their sides, their legs fused to together, and their skin turned a sickening white. These were only some of the physical changes. There were more. They were dangerous. And all creatures need to eat. Ultimately, the lake became their habitation, according to Uncle Caleb, except on extremely rainy nights, when the land can become as wet as any lake. One part that Uncle Caleb left out, however, was that Captain Feester asked Nick's great-grandfather to keep the land for him should he leave and make sure it is undisturbed for as long as possible, even passing it down through generations to ensure this. Eventually, Captain Feester, like his daughters and wife (who did not change, by the way) disappeared. There is also a backstory that is so subtly intertwined that you don't really notice it until the end of the story. Uncle Caleb was, and is, in love with a girl he has known since childhood named Dorothy. Dorothy does not return his affection and marries his best friend, Holmes. When Holmes dies (of pneumonia), and she is widowed, she chooses another man (an "outsider" as the family phrases it), adding insult to injury. Where this intertwines is that when the man she marries is killed (on a rainy night), Caleb is the only suspect. It was a reasonable choice since after Dorothy married for the second time (and not to Caleb) he fell apart and moved into the farmhouse near Howard's Lake. He was never convicted of any crime, but the legend of the Sturkeyville Butcher was in place and many considered this legend and Caleb one and the same. Nick was not one of these, but had seen his uncle's decline. Not only with the drinking, but with the newly formed conviction of the existence of the feesters. In addition, after Dorothy's death (natural causes) he moved into the old Feester house. The deterioration continues in all ways that statement implies. The story is a masterwork of horror and the finale was as terrifying as anything I have read by Lovecraft. Absolutely brilliant.

*End of Spoilers*

I am saddened by the fact that no new stories will be forthcoming from Bob Leman (the man having passed away before this book came out). I am further saddened that in his lifetime he was not given the recognition and accolades he obviously deserved. This collection is his memoriam and Midnight House has done a wonderful job. All lovers of fantasy, sci-fi, or horror should read this collection, absorb it, make it a part of you, and then spread the message to others. As he wrote his stories, so should we read them; as a labor of love.
top_profane.title: The Best Horror Writer No One Has Ever Heard Of

top_profane.text:
Note: I would like to thank author Rebecca Morris, NetGalley, and WildBlue Press for a free ARC in exchange for an honest review of this book. I'm an avid reader of the true crime genre and I'm always looking forward to getting my hands on another book!

Richard "Dick" Kitchel was a 17-year-old high school student who never returned from a party in Corvallis, Oregon in 1967. His body was found 10 days later floating in the Willamette River. It was clear that he had been strangled and that some sort of struggle occurred before his body was dumped in the river (the strangulation killed him and there was no water in his lungs, ruling out drowning). The condition of the body, having been exposed to the river's elements and fish, was not helpful in determining much about the murder. Similarly, since it had been 10 days and no one really knew Dick had been murdered (most young teens have willingly left home, according to the detectives in the 1960s) any potential crime scenes also lacked physical evidence.

Detectives relied heavily on testimonies of individuals that had seen Dick that night. They began to piece together information, listened to various theories about what could have happened to Dick, but unfortunately... the case went cold.

Author Rebecca Morris gives a voice to Dick, opening up the case and discussing what she recalled at the time of the murders. This did indeed happen in her hometown and she knew several of the individuals that were involved in the investigation. Towards the end of the book, it becomes clear that 40 years later another cold case investigator began working on the case. While there is a strong belief that (someone whose name I will not spoil here!) was involved in Dick's murder (even if accidental)... the case still has not been officially solved (this isn't a spoiler).

There are some great things about this book. First and foremost, I really do appreciate when local individuals get involved with investigations because people within the community often have access to testimony that investigators may not. A murderer is more likely to tell their barber a key piece of evidence as opposed to a detective. Also, authors that are writing about their hometowns and/or events that took place as they lived through them are often not as cold as "outsiders" who tend to give a lot of fact, but not much "heart" when discussing crime.

Secondly, I always enjoy (which is an awkward word when discussing true crime) hearing stories where silenced victims get a chance to have a voice. It seems like a lot of people in Corvallis were perfectly fine with Dick's death--he was, perhaps, a bit of a troublemaker. He liked to drink (this was common for teens in the 1960s). He had anger issues and he and his father were often fighting. He had bullied at least his stepbrother and probably others. So on the surface, sure, maybe you think that you wouldn't miss Dick much either. BUT there are other things about Dick that Morris makes more apparent so you can understand that even if Dick was a dick (I couldn't resist), he still didn't deserve to be murdered. His case *should* be solved.

However, there are some "issues" that I have with this book that I deducted some stars for when I decided to sit down and rate the book. Please note, I think a 3-star review is an "average" review and think that "average" books are perfectly acceptable and still worth reading. I'd suggest that in my opinion, about 90% of books out there are 3-star books. It takes a little more to get me to give 4 or 5 stars.

My biggest issue was that Morris, at times, was a little too close to traveling down the memoir road. While I appreciate her insight, and it is needed, there were often times (entire chapters, really) where she was discussing her own views of Corvallis and/or her own experiences--and I felt like there were times where Dick and his murder were completely absent. Some background information about an author is good, especially when they were around during the events and want to establish themselves within the story. However... too much makes a reader begin to wonder... how much of this is 'true crime' and how much of this is 'memoir'? I think areas where the book falls too far into memoir (and information about Morris) are tempting to skip over, and distract from the story I came to read about--Dick.

Still, this book does a decent job of explaining why Dick's case remains unsolved. There are several factors--the most important seemingly being that there is a lack of physical evidence. I, perhaps like the author, feel like detectives/investigators could have done a bit more to get the case solved. After all... they have a strong inclination about who murdered Dick, but never fully pursued that avenue. It is unfortunate, because Dick's case does deserve some closure. But perhaps this book is the first step towards that closure, because now readers know is story.
top_profane.title: More of a memoir than true-crime, but interesting nonetheless!
